subject: Cardiology
title: Abdominal aortic aneurysm
preambleMarkdown:
# Abdominal aortic aneurysm

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) means permanent focal dilation of the abdominal aorta.
- Most AAAs are infrarenal, between the renal arteries and aortic bifurcation.
- Most patients are asymptomatic until screening, incidental imaging, expansion, or rupture.
- The dangerous event is rupture, which can cause rapid retroperitoneal or intraperitoneal hemorrhage.
- Exams usually test:
  - Who should be screened.
  - Which imaging test to choose.
  - When surveillance is enough.
  - When repair is indicated.
  - How to manage suspected rupture without delaying definitive care.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Smoking is the strongest modifiable risk factor.
- Ultrasonography is the usual screening and surveillance test.
- CTA defines anatomy and rupture in stable patients.
- Suspected rupture with instability requires immediate vascular surgical action, not a prolonged diagnostic workup.
- Repair decisions depend on size, symptoms, growth, anatomy, operative risk, life expectancy, and sex-specific considerations.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- AAA is usually defined on imaging as:
  - Abdominal aortic diameter of at least 3.0 cm, or
  - Focal dilation at least 50% greater than expected for that segment.
- The normal adult infrarenal aorta is roughly 2 cm in diameter.
- Diameter, symptoms, growth rate, and anatomy drive management.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Location: infrarenal, juxtarenal, pararenal, or suprarenal.
- Morphology: fusiform or saccular.
- Clinical state: asymptomatic, symptomatic, contained rupture, or free rupture.
- Etiology: degenerative, inflammatory, infectious, traumatic, anastomotic, or connective-tissue associated.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk increases with age.
- AAA is uncommon before age 50.
- Classic screening population:
  - Older men.
  - History of smoking.
- AAA is more common in men.
- Women have lower prevalence but may rupture at smaller diameters.
- Important risk associations:
  - Smoking.
  - Hypertension.
  - Atherosclerotic disease.
  - Family history.
  - Connective-tissue disease.
  - Prior aneurysm in another vascular bed.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Most AAAs occur below the renal arteries.
- Important anatomic descriptors:
  - Infrarenal.
  - Juxtarenal.
  - Pararenal.
  - Suprarenal.
  - Iliac involvement.
- Anatomy determines:
  - Whether endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR) is feasible.
  - Where graft landing zones can be placed.
  - Risk to renal, mesenteric, spinal, or iliac perfusion.
  - Whether open repair is required.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The abdominal aorta carries high-pressure systemic arterial flow.
- As the aneurysm enlarges:
  - Wall tension increases.
  - Rupture risk rises.
  - Mural thrombus may form.
  - Distal embolization can occur.
- Rupture causes:
  - Sudden blood loss.
  - Hypovolemic shock.
  - Syncope or cardiovascular collapse.
- Retroperitoneal bleeding may briefly tamponade, which can create a transiently stable presentation.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Most AAAs result from progressive weakening of the aortic wall.
- Common mechanisms include:
  - Degeneration of extracellular matrix.
  - Chronic inflammation.
  - Atherosclerotic risk-factor burden.
  - Smoking-related vascular injury.
  - Genetic or connective-tissue vulnerability in selected patients.
- Complications occur when the aneurysm:
  - Expands and becomes painful.
  - Ruptures.
  - Embolizes thrombus distally.
  - Compresses adjacent structures.
  - Becomes infected or inflammatory.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Degenerative AAA is most common.
- Major contributors:
  - Smoking.
  - Hypertension.
  - Age-related medial degeneration.
  - Atherosclerotic disease burden.
- Less common causes:
  - Trauma.
  - Vasculitis.
  - Mycotic or infected aneurysm.
  - Anastomotic aneurysm after vascular surgery.
  - Connective-tissue disorders.
  - Syphilitic or other inflammatory aortitis, more classically thoracic but possible in broader aortic disease.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Strong risk factors:
  - Older age.
  - Male sex.
  - Smoking history.
  - First-degree family history of AAA.
- Additional risk factors:
  - Hypertension.
  - Atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease.
  - Hyperlipidemia.
  - Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
  - Other aneurysms.
  - Connective-tissue disease features.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Family history increases AAA risk.
- Suspect a heritable aortopathy when aneurysm occurs with:
  - Young age.
  - Multiple aneurysm sites.
  - Strong family clustering.
  - Features of Marfan syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome, vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, or related connective-tissue disease.
- First-degree relatives may need screening depending on age, sex, family pattern, and local guidance.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Infection is not the usual cause of AAA.
- Consider infected or mycotic aneurysm when there is:
  - Fever.
  - Bacteremia.
  - Immunosuppression.
  - Recent endovascular or vascular procedure.
  - Rapid aneurysm enlargement.
  - Periaortic inflammatory changes on imaging.
- Infected aneurysm requires urgent specialist management because rupture risk can be high.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Incidental imaging finding.
- Pulsatile abdominal mass.
- Abdominal, flank, groin, or back pain.
- Syncope, hypotension, shock, or cardiac arrest with rupture.
- Distal embolization causing acute limb or toe ischemia.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about:
  - Known prior AAA size and surveillance history.
  - New abdominal, flank, back, groin, buttock, or leg pain.
  - Syncope, near-syncope, weakness, or collapse.
  - Smoking history.
  - Family history of AAA or sudden unexplained death.
  - Prior vascular surgery or EVAR.
  - Fever or bacteremia symptoms.
  - Anticoagulant or antiplatelet use.
  - Symptoms of limb ischemia.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess immediately for shock:
  - Hypotension.
  - Tachycardia.
  - Altered mental status.
  - Cool extremities.
  - Poor perfusion.
- Abdominal findings:
  - Pulsatile abdominal mass may be present.
  - Tenderness may suggest expansion or rupture.
  - A normal exam does not exclude AAA.
- Vascular findings:
  - Check femoral and distal pulses.
  - Look for acute limb ischemia or embolic toe findings.
- Skin findings in retroperitoneal bleeding:
  - Flank ecchymosis can occur.
  - Periumbilical ecchymosis can occur.
  - These signs are late and insensitive.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Known AAA with new pain.
- Pain with hypotension or syncope.
- Pulsatile mass with abdominal or back pain.
- Rapid expansion.
- Fever or bacteremia with aneurysm.
- New limb ischemia.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Dangerous mimics:
  - Aortic dissection.
  - Ruptured visceral aneurysm.
  - Acute coronary syndrome.
  - Mesenteric ischemia.
  - Perforated viscus.
  - Severe gastrointestinal bleeding.
- Common mimics:
  - Renal colic.
  - Pyelonephritis.
  - Diverticulitis.
  - Pancreatitis.
  - Musculoskeletal back pain.
  - Hernia.
- Do not anchor on benign back pain in an older smoker with hypotension, syncope, or abdominal pain.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use ultrasound to detect or surveil an infrarenal AAA.
- Use CTA for stable symptomatic patients and operative planning.
- Do not delay definitive repair in a profoundly unstable patient with compelling rupture.
- Obtain CBC, renal function, coagulation studies, lactate, type and crossmatch when they do not delay care.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Screening or surveillance:
  - Abdominal ultrasound is the usual first-line test.
  - It measures diameter and tracks growth.
- Stable symptomatic patient:
  - CTA abdomen/pelvis defines rupture, leak, branch involvement, iliac extension, and repair anatomy.
- Unstable suspected rupture:
  - Do not delay vascular surgery or operative/endovascular management for complete imaging.
  - Bedside ultrasound can support the diagnosis if immediately available.
- Labs support resuscitation but do not diagnose AAA:
  - CBC.
  - Renal function.
  - Coagulation studies.
  - Lactate.
  - Type and crossmatch.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis is imaging-based.
- Typical diagnostic threshold:
  - Abdominal aorta diameter at least 3.0 cm.
- Rupture is suggested by:
  - AAA plus hemodynamic instability.
  - Retroperitoneal hematoma or contrast extravasation on CT.
  - Severe abdominal, back, flank, or groin pain with shock in a patient with known AAA.
- Symptomatic aneurysm is clinically important even without free rupture.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Low immediate risk:
  - Small asymptomatic AAA.
  - Stable diameter on surveillance.
- Higher risk:
  - Larger diameter.
  - Rapid growth.
  - Symptoms.
  - Saccular morphology.
  - Female sex at a given size.
  - Family or syndromic aortopathy.
- Emergency:
  - Suspected rupture.
  - Hemodynamic instability.
  - Severe new pain with known AAA.
  - Distal embolization or limb ischemia.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- If rupture is possible:
  - Call vascular surgery immediately.
  - Activate operating room or endovascular team.
  - Alert anesthesia and blood bank.
  - Place large-bore IV access.
  - Prepare blood products.
  - Avoid delaying definitive care for nonessential tests.
- If stable:
  - Confirm size and anatomy.
  - Review prior imaging.
  - Assess operative risk and life expectancy.
  - Arrange vascular follow-up.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Smoking cessation is the most important modifiable intervention.
- Control cardiovascular risk:
  - Blood pressure.
  - Lipids.
  - Diabetes when present.
  - Exercise and diet as tolerated.
- Avoid missed surveillance imaging.
- Counsel patients to seek urgent care for new abdominal, flank, back, groin pain, syncope, or collapse.

### Pharmacological Management

- No medication reliably reverses an established AAA.
- Use medications to reduce overall cardiovascular risk:
  - Antihypertensives when indicated.
  - Statin therapy when ASCVD risk supports it.
  - Antiplatelet therapy when indicated for other vascular disease.
- Treat pain carefully in symptomatic patients.
- Antibiotics are needed only when infected aneurysm or another infection is suspected.

### Procedures

- Surveillance imaging:
  - Ultrasound for many stable infrarenal AAAs.
  - CTA when anatomy, symptoms, or repair planning require detail.
- Repair options:
  - EVAR through femoral arterial access when anatomy is suitable.
  - Open surgical repair with graft replacement when EVAR is unsuitable or durability is prioritized.
- EVAR requires long-term surveillance for:
  - Endoleak.
  - Sac enlargement.
  - Graft migration.
  - Limb occlusion.

### Surgical Management

- Rupture: activate vascular surgery, anesthesia, blood bank, and operating or endovascular resources.
- Use large-bore access and blood-product resuscitation when hemorrhage is suspected.
- Surveillance: smoking cessation, cardiovascular risk reduction, and interval imaging based on diameter.
- Definitive repair is by EVAR or open repair depending on anatomy, urgency, risk, and long-term surveillance feasibility.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stable unrepaired AAA:
  - Track maximum diameter.
  - Track growth rate.
  - Confirm surveillance interval.
  - Reassess symptoms at each visit.
- After EVAR:
  - Monitor for endoleak.
  - Monitor sac size.
  - Monitor graft position and limb patency.
- After open repair:
  - Monitor graft complications.
  - Monitor cardiovascular risk.
  - Assess wound and functional recovery.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Follow-up should document:
  - Current AAA diameter.
  - Growth compared with prior imaging.
  - Symptoms.
  - Smoking status.
  - Blood pressure and ASCVD risk management.
  - Next imaging date.
  - Vascular surgery plan.
- Missed surveillance can allow silent enlargement before rupture.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Rupture with hemorrhagic shock.
- Contained leak.
- Distal thromboembolism.
- Acute limb ischemia.
- Aortoenteric fistula, especially after prior repair.
- Infected aneurysm.
- EVAR complications:
  - Endoleak.
  - Migration.
  - Graft limb thrombosis.
  - Sac enlargement.
- Open repair complications:
  - Bleeding.
  - Renal injury.
  - Bowel ischemia.
  - Myocardial infarction.
  - Graft infection.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on:
  - Aneurysm size.
  - Growth rate.
  - Symptoms.
  - Rupture status.
  - Age and frailty.
  - Kidney function.
  - Coronary and pulmonary disease.
  - Repair anatomy.
  - Access to emergency vascular care.
- Elective repair has much lower mortality than ruptured AAA repair.
- Ruptured AAA remains highly lethal even with treatment.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Primary prevention:
  - Avoid smoking.
  - Treat hypertension.
  - Manage ASCVD risk.
- Secondary prevention:
  - Stop smoking after diagnosis.
  - Keep surveillance appointments.
  - Treat associated vascular disease.
  - Screen selected relatives when indicated.
- Screening prevents rupture by finding silent AAA before symptoms occur.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain:
  - AAA is a widened section of the abdominal aorta.
  - Small AAAs are often watched with scheduled imaging.
  - Larger, fast-growing, or symptomatic AAAs may need repair.
  - Smoking increases growth and rupture risk.
- Seek emergency care for:
  - Sudden abdominal pain.
  - Back, flank, groin, or leg pain.
  - Fainting or near-fainting.
  - Sudden weakness or collapse.
  - Cold, painful, or pale limb.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- AAA is rare in children.
- Consider unusual causes:
  - Connective-tissue disease.
  - Vasculitis.
  - Infection.
  - Trauma.
  - Prior vascular procedure.
- Pediatric vascular or genetic specialist input is needed.

### Pregnancy

- AAA in pregnancy is uncommon but high risk when present.
- Maternal stabilization comes first in rupture.
- Coordinate:
  - Vascular surgery.
  - Maternal-fetal medicine.
  - Anesthesia.
  - ICU.
- Consider heritable aortopathy when aneurysm is present in a young pregnant patient.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal impairment affects:
  - CTA contrast risk.
  - EVAR planning.
  - Perioperative risk.
  - Medication dosing.
- Do not delay life-saving imaging or repair for contrast concerns when rupture is likely.
- Use renal-protective planning when the patient is stable.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Typical site: infrarenal aorta.
- Rupture triad is pain, hypotension, and pulsatile mass, but the full triad is often absent.
- Commonly tested elective threshold in average-risk men is about 5.5 cm, interpreted in context.
- EVAR requires lifelong surveillance.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
<!-- source: lib.md import start -->
Source: `/Users/musaab/Downloads/lib.md`. Imported on 2026-07-14.

### Source Excerpt: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm

_Source lines: 4489-4588._

```text
Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm
A. General Characteristics

1. Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is an abnormal localized
dilation of the aorta. Most AAAs occur between the renal arteries
and iliac bifurcation.

2. The incidence increases with age. AAAs are rare before the age of
50 years. The average age at time of diagnosis is 65 to 70 years.

3. AAAs are much more common in men, though more likely to
rupture in women at a given size.

B. Causes
1. Multifactorial-in most cases, there is atherosclerotic weakening of
the aortic wall.

2. Other predisposing factors include trauma, HTN, vasculitis,
smoking, and positive family history.

3. Syphilis and connective tissue abnormalities (e.g., Marfan disease)
are associated with thoracic aneurysms, but they may involve the
lower aorta as well.

C. Clinical Features
1. Usually asymptomatic and discovered on either abdominal
examination or a radiologic study done for another reason
2. Sense of "fullness"
3. Pain may or may not be present-if present, located in the
hypogastrium and lower back and usually throbbing in character
4. Pulsatile mass on abdominal examination
5. Symptoms suggesting expansion and impending rupture include the
following:
a. Sudden onset of severe pain in the back or lower abdomen,
radiating to the groin, buttocks, or legs
b. Grey Turner sign (ecchymoses on back and flanks) and Cullen sign
(ecchymoses around umbilicus) are signs of retroperitoneal
bleeding. Neither is very sensitive for ruptured AAA

## 6. Rupture of an AAA

a. The triad of abdominal pain, hypotension, and a palpable
pulsatile abdominal mass indicates a ruptured AAA and
emergent laparotomy is indicated. No further diagnostic testing
is needed with this constellation of symptoms; however,
patients may present with only one or two components of this
triad
b. Cardiovascular collapse
c. Syncope or near-syncope, secondary to sudden hemorrhage
d. Nausea and vomiting
D. Diagnosis

## 1. Ultrasound

a. Test of choice to evaluate both the location and size of the
aneurysm
b. 100% sensitive in detecting AAAs
2. CT scan
a. 100% sensitive in detecting AAAs
b. Takes longer to perform than plain radiographs or ultrasound;
should only be used in hemodynamically stable patients
c. CT is the scan of choice for preoperative planning
Quick HIT
Leriche syndrome: Atheromatous occlusion of distal aorta just
above bifurcation causing bilateral claudication, impotence, and
absent/diminished femoral pulses.

E. Treatment
1. Unruptured aneurysms
a. Management largely depends on size of the aneurysm.

- Data from the ADAM trial and UK-SAT have shown that if
the aneurysm is >5 cm in diameter or symptomatic, surgical
resection with synthetic graft placement is recommended.

(The infrarenal aorta is replaced with a fabric tube.) This
can often be done endovascularly by accessing the femoral
artery. The diameter of the normal adult infrarenal aorta is
about 2 cm.

- The management of asymptomatic aneurysms <5 cm is
controversial. Periodic imaging is recommended to follow up
growth. Growth of more than 1 cm in a year suggests
elevated risk. No "safe" size exists however, and small AAAs
can rupture.

d. Other factors to consider are the patient's life expectancy
(patient may be more likely to die of other medical illnesses),
and the risk of surgery.

2. Ruptured AAAs: Emergency surgical repair is indicated. All of these
patients are unstable.

a. While open repair remains the gold standard, some ruptured
AAAs may be repaired endovascularly as well with stents.
```

<!-- source: lib.md import end -->

- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
